Client 2: And I want it simple. I’m getting close to retiring and I don’t want surprises, especially with healthcare.
Advisor: That makes sense. Michae, why don’t we start with the big picture cash flow—about how much gross income is coming into the household in a year?
Client 1: All-in, household gross is 224300 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And that’s from a mix of your self-employed work plus other income streams, right?
Client 1: Yeah. I’ve got a weekly bonus that totals 50400 annually, and then rental income that’s monthly—173850 a year.

Advisor: Great. Let’s talk spending next—what do your total monthly expenses usually run, just ballpark?
Client 2: It feels like… around thirteen thousand a month.
Advisor: When you say thirteen thousand, do you mean 13000 total monthly expenses including the mortgage and everything?
Client 2: Yes—13000 total monthly expenses.
Advisor: Okay. Now debts—what are the monthly payments you’re committed to, total, across the mortgage and any other loans?

Client 2: Yes—13000 total monthly expenses.
Advisor: Okay. Now debts—what are the monthly payments you’re committed to, total, across the mortgage and any other loans?
Client 1: The mortgage is 3150 a month, and there’s another loan that’s 200 a month, so 3350 total monthly debt payments.
Advisor: One sec—just noting that. On the mortgage itself, what’s the current balance and rate?
Client 1: Balance is 89500 and the interest rate is 4.8%.

Client 2: The balance is 3050, the interest rate is 23.18%, and the final payment date is 2028-07-01.
Advisor: Thanks. Switching to assets—roughly how much do you have invested that’s not the house? What’s the total investable assets number you track?
Client 1: Total investable assets is 1222500.
Advisor: Okay. Let’s break that down a bit so I understand where things sit. Do you have a joint taxable brokerage account?
Client 1: Yes—joint taxable brokerage, it’s 765000.

Client 1: There’s a small private markets thing at the bank—10150.
Advisor: Okay. And then the home—just so we have it on the balance sheet—what do you estimate the primary residence value at?
Client 2: We use 314100 for the primary residence value.
Advisor: Thanks. Protection planning—do either of you have long-term care coverage in place?
Client 1: I do. Long-term care policy—monthly premium is 500, coverage amount is 708300, and it’s covered until 2049-11-29.

Client 2: Yes, use 314100 for the property value.
Advisor: Perfect—thank you. Now on the debt side, you gave me the mortgage and that other loan. Do you track a combined total for loans outstanding across everything?
Client 1: Yeah, total loans outstanding is 92600.
Advisor: Okay. And just to split that total—mortgage balance outstanding is still 89500, right?
Client 1: Correct, mortgage balance outstanding is 89500.

Client 1: The mortgage is 3150 a month, and there’s another loan that’s 200 a month, so 3350 total monthly debt payments.
Advisor: One sec—just noting that. On the mortgage itself, what’s the current balance and rate?
Client 1: Balance is 89500 and the interest rate is 4.8%.
Advisor: And do you know the scheduled final payment date on that mortgage?
Client 1: Yes—2028-10-17.

Client 1: Yes—2028-10-17.
Advisor: Great. And the other loan—what’s the balance, rate, and when it’s supposed to be paid off?
Client 2: The balance is 3050, the interest rate is 23.18%, and the final payment date is 2028-07-01.
Advisor: Thanks. Switching to assets—roughly how much do you have invested that’s not the house? What’s the total investable assets number you track?
Client 1: Total investable assets is 1222500.

Client 2: Just Michae’s for now.
Advisor: Okay—so to summarize what I heard: income includes the weekly bonus and rental income on Michae’s side plus Margaret’s wages; expenses are steady; debts are the mortgage and that smaller loan; and assets are spread across taxable, retirement, cash-like, and a small private holding, plus the home. Did I miss anything major you expected to cover today?
Client 1: No, that’s the core of it. Next I want to talk about how conservative we can stay without falling behind.
Client 2: Yes—like what happens if the market drops right when I retire.
Advisor: Got it. Michae, Margaret—before we get into the “how conservative can we be” part, I just want to make sure the balance sheet totals tie out cleanly.

Advisor: Hi Michae, hi Margaret—good to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into the weeds, what would make today’s meeting a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks. Honestly, I want to know we’re not missing anything big—retirement timing, taxes, and just… downside protection.
Client 2: And I want it simple. I’m getting close to retiring and I don’t want surprises, especially with healthcare.
Advisor: That makes sense. Michae, why don’t we start with the big picture cash flow—about how much gross income is coming into the household in a year?
Client 1: All-in, household gross is 224300 a year.

Advisor: Hi Michae, hi Margaret—good to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into the weeds, what would make today’s meeting a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks. Honestly, I want to know we’re not missing anything big—retirement timing, taxes, and just… downside protection.
Client 2: And I want it simple. I’m getting close to retiring and I don’t want surprises, especially with healthcare.
Advisor: That makes sense. Michae, why don’t we start with the big picture cash flow—about how much gross income is coming into the household in a year?

Client 2: Exactly. I just want it simple and reliable.
Advisor: Okay—thanks. Before we run the stress test, I want to lock down a couple household basics so I don’t make assumptions.
Advisor: Michae and Margaret, are you two married?
Client 1: Yes, we’re married.
Advisor: Got it. And household size-wise—just confirming—how many adults are in the household?

Advisor: Got it—thank you. And that’s from a mix of your self-employed work plus other income streams, right?
Client 1: Yeah. I’ve got a weekly bonus that totals 50400 annually, and then rental income that’s monthly—173850 a year.
Advisor: Okay, helpful. Margaret, and you’re still working—what’s your gross pay roughly?
Client 2: Mine is 82950 gross per year.
Advisor: Perfect. And Michae, your self-employed gross—what do you show annually before taxes?

Client 2: And I want it simple. I’m getting close to retiring and I don’t want surprises, especially with healthcare.

Client 1: Yeah. I’ve got a weekly bonus that totals 50400 annually, and then rental income that’s monthly—173850 a year.

Client 2: No—number of dependents is 0.
Advisor: Okay. When we model taxes, I also want to make sure I’m using the right bracket assumption. Do you know what bracket you tend to fall into—ballpark is fine?
Client 1: It’s the 37% bracket.
Advisor: Alright, 37%. And last admin-ish one: how did you come to work with me—are you looking for an ongoing relationship with a registered investment advisor, or more of a one-time plan?
Client 2: Ongoing. Advisory channel is RIA (registered investment advisor).
